items.q: On a traffic-light label, what does a red light indicate?
items.correct: High amounts of that nutrient per 100g - eat less often.
items.misconception: The food is dangerous and should never be eaten.
items.why: Students treat red as 'forbidden'. Traffic lights are a guidance system - red = high, eat less often or in small portions. Many foods (cheese, olive oil) are red on certain nutrients but fine in moderation.

items.q: Which vitamin is most likely to be deficient in a poorly planned vegan diet?
items.why: Students guess vitamin C (it's the famous one), but plant foods are rich in C. B12 is found almost exclusively in animal products, so vegans need fortified foods or supplements.

items.q: What does freezing do to bacteria in food?
items.correct: Makes them dormant (slows or halts growth), but does not kill them.
items.misconception: Kills all bacteria in the food.
items.why: Students think freezing sterilises food. It just stops bacteria multiplying - they revive when thawed. That's why thawed food should be cooked promptly and not refrozen raw.

items.q: Which role of egg is being used when brushed onto pastry tops?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Glazing. “Binding” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: Strengthens the gluten network. “Has no role” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Typical salt level in bread as percentage of flour weight?
items.why: The real figure is 1. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
